zh.lifeSummary: 當Lord Robert de Swinburne of Little Horkesley Sir Knight於1357年出生在英國諾圖波藍德卡亞頓時，他的父親William de Swinburne88歲，母親Philippa de Gernon88歲。他於1357年8月之前與Agnes de Felton在英國英格蘭結婚。他們至少是1個兒子和3個女兒的父母。他去世於1391年10月6日在英國艾可蕾托 霍可斯利享年34歲，埋葬在英國英格蘭艾可蕾托 霍可斯利。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Lord Robert de Swinburne of Little Horkesley Sir Knight于1357年出生在英国諾圖波藍德卡亞頓时，他的父亲William de Swinburne88岁，母亲Philippa de Gernon88岁。他于1357年8月之前与Agnes de Felton在英国英格兰结婚。他们至少是1个儿子和3个女儿的父母。他去世于1391年10月6日在英国艾可蕾托 霍可斯利享年34岁，埋葬在英国英格兰艾可蕾托 霍可斯利。

nameMeanings.name: Knight
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: status or occupational name from Middle English knight ‘retainer, attendant’ (Old English cniht ‘boy, youth, lad)’. The specialized feudal sense ‘a high-ranking tenant bound to serve his lord as a mounted soldier’ is not known to have ever given rise to the surname, although it is not out of the question that it may occasionally have been used as a nickname, perhaps for someone who played the part of an armed knight in a local pageant.
Irish: sometimes adopted for Gaelic Mac an Ridire ‘son of the knight’. See also McKnight .

variants.name: Knights
meanings: English (East Anglia): variant of Knight , with post-medieval excrescent -s.

variants.name: McKnight
meanings: Irish: part translation of Gaelic Mac an Ridire ‘son of the horseman (Gaelic ridire)’, formerly Anglicized McRuddery.
Scottish (Dumfriesshire and Galloway) and Irish (Ulster): Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Neachtain, which is usually Anglicized as McNaughton .

nameMeanings.name: Lord
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Pet form of   Lorraine   or variant spelling of   Laurie .

variants.name: Lorraine
meanings: Transferred use of the surname, in origin denoting a migrant from the province of Lorraine in eastern France. This derives its name from Latin Lotharingia ‘territory of the people of Lothar’. The latter is a Germanic personal name derived from hlud ‘fame’ + heri, hari ‘army’. Lorraine began to be used as a girl's name in Scotland in the 19th century, and for a time in the second half of the 20th century enjoyed great popularity, which has since waned.

nameMeanings.name: Robert
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the many French names of Germanic origin that were introduced into Britain by the Normans; it has since remained in continuous use. It is derived from the nearly synonymous elements hrōd ‘fame’ + berht ‘bright, famous’, and had a native Old English predecessor of similar form (Hreodbeorht), which was supplanted by the Norman name. Two dukes of Normandy in the 11th century bore the name: the father of William the Conqueror (sometimes identified with the legendary Robert the Devil), and his eldest son. It was borne also by three kings of Scotland, notably Robert the Bruce ( 1274–1329 ), who freed Scotland from English domination. The altered short form Bob is very common, but Hob and Dob, which were common in the Middle Ages and gave rise to surnames, are extinct. See also Rupert .

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Short form of the various boys' names with the first syllable Ed-, especially Edward .

variants.name: Edward
meanings: From an Old English personal name derived from ēad ‘prosperity, riches’ + weard ‘guard’. This has been one of the most successful of all Old English names, in frequent use from before the Conquest to the present day, and even being exported into other European languages. It was the name of three Anglo-Saxon kings and has been borne by eight kings of England since the Norman Conquest. It is also the name of the youngest son of Queen Elizabeth II . The most influential early bearer was King Edward the Confessor ( ?1002–66 ; ruled 1042–66 ). In a troubled period of English history, he contrived to rule fairly and (for a time at any rate) firmly. But in the latter part of his reign he paid more attention to his religion than to his kingdom. He died childless, and his death sparked off conflicting claims to his throne, which were resolved by the victory of William the Conqueror at the Battle of Hastings. His memory was honoured by Normans and English alike, for his fairness and his piety. Edward's mother was Norman; he had spent part of his youth in Normandy; and William claimed to have been nominated by Edward as his successor. Edward was canonized in the 12th century, and came to be venerated throughout Europe as a model of a Christian king.

nameMeanings.name: Swinburne
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (Durham): habitational name primarily from Great and Little Swinburne (Northumberland), but perhaps also occasionally from one or other places similarly named, from Old English swīn ‘pig’ + burna ‘stream’.

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Short form of Oswald or any of the various other names beginning with Os-.

nameMeanings.name: Little
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: nickname for a small man (if not ironic), or distinguishing epithet for the younger of two bearers of the same personal name, from Middle English littel, Old English lȳtel (see Light 3).
Irish: translation into English of Gaelic Ó Beagáin ‘descendant of Beagán’ (see Began ).
Americanized form (translation into English) of any of various European surnames meaning ‘little’, e.g. French Petit , Polish Mały (see Maly 1).

variants.name: Litle
meanings: English: variant of   Little  . This surname is very rare in Britain.

variants.name: Littles
meanings: English: variant of   Little  .

variants.name: Light
meanings: English: from Middle English liht ‘light, active’ (Old English lēoht).
English: topographic name from Middle English liht, leht ‘light’ (Old English lēoht), used for a ‘light place, clearing, glade’.
English: nickname from Middle English lit ‘little’ (Old English lȳt). Compare Little .

variants.name: Littell
meanings: English: variant of   Little  .

variants.name: Lytle
meanings: Transferred use of the mainly Scottish surname, in origin a local name for someone who came ‘from the island’ (Anglo-Norman de l'isle). (The island in question would in many cases have been an area of higher, dry ground in a marsh or fen, rather than in a sea or river.) There may have been some confusion with Lyall .

variants.name: Lyttle
meanings: Irish (mainly northern Ireland): variant of English   Little  .

variants.name: Littel
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Michel.
English: variant of   Little  .
South German (Bavaria): unexplained. This surname is also found in France (mainly Alsace).

variants.name: Petit
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French/Haitian Alain, Michel, Normand, Mireille, Philippe, Pierre, Achille, Armand, Jean Claude, Jean Jacques, Ketly, Adrien.

French, Walloon, Catalan, English, Jewish (from southern France), West Indian (mainly Haiti), and Mauritian: from (Old) French, Catalan, and Middle English petit ‘small’, hence a nickname for a small man (or an ironic nickname for a big man), or a distinguishing name for the younger of two bearers of the same personal name. Compare Little , Petee , and Petite .

History: Charles Petit from Anneville-Ambourville in Seine-Maritime, France, married Jeanne Rossignol (Martin and Renée Desjardins) in Quebec City, QC, in 1670. Nicolas Petit dit Lapré/Laprée from Le Gué-d'Alleré in Charente-Maritime, France, married Marie Pouponnel in Trois-Rivières, QC, in 1656. Pierre Petit dit Gobin from Lyon in Rhône married Marguerite Véron in Trois-Rivières, QC, in 1692. Pierre Petit dit Saint-Pierre from Évreux in Eure, France, married Judith Miville in Quebec City, QC, in 1758; see also St. Pierre . — This surname is listed in the register of Huguenot ancestors recognized by the Huguenot Society of America and also in the similar register of the Huguenot Society of South Carolina.

variants.name: Lighthill
meanings: English: variant of   Little  , modified so as to resemble a placename.

nameMeanings.name: Sir
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Short form of Simon , now rare, but more common in the Middle Ages, when it gave rise to the surnames Simms and Simpson.

variants.name: Simon
meanings: Usual English form of Simeon , borne in the New Testament by various characters: two apostles, a brother of Jesus, a Pharisee, a leper, a tanner, a sorcerer (who offered money for the gifts of the Holy Ghost, giving rise to the term simony), and the man who carried Jesus's cross to the Crucifixion.

historicEvents.title: Wycliffe's Bible
historicEvents.description: John Wycliffe, English theologian and professor at the University of Oxford, completed a translation of the Bible from the Latin Vulgate into Middle English. This translation is known as Wycliffe's Bible and was completed around 1382.

en.lifeSummary: When Lord Robert de Swinburne of Little Horkesley Sir Knight was born in 1357, in Capheaton, Northumberland, England, his father, William de Swinburne, was 88 and his mother, Philippa de Gernon, was 88. He married Agnes de Felton before August 1357, in England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 1 son and 3 daughters. He died on 6 October 1391, in Little Horkesley, Essex, England, at the age of 34, and was buried in Little Horkesley, Essex, England, United Kingdom.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Lord Robert de Swinburne of Little Horkesley Sir Knight est né en 1357, à Capheaton, Northumberland, Angleterre, son père, William de Swinburne, avait 88 ans et sa mère, Philippa de Gernon, avait 88 ans. Il a épousé Agnes de Felton avant août 1357, à Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Ils ont eu au moins 1 fils et 3 filles. Il est décédé le 6 octobre 1391, à Little Horkesley, Essex, Angleterre, à l’âge de 34 ans, et a été enterré à Little Horkesley, Essex, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni.

Lui sposato Agnes de Felton prima del agosto 1357, nel Inghilterra, Regno Unito. Erano genitori di almeno 1 figlio e 3 figlie. ja.lifeSummary: Lord Robert de Swinburne of Little Horkesley Sir Knightが1357年にイギリスノースウンバーランドカフエアトンで生まれた時，父William de Swinburneは88歳，母Philippa de Gernonは88歳であった。1357年8月以前にイギリスイギリスでAgnes de Feltonと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも1人の息子と3人の娘の親であった。彼は1391年10月6日にイギリスエセックスリットル ホールケスリーで34歳で死去し，イギリスイギリスエセックスリットル ホールケスリーにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: Lord Robert de Swinburne of Little Horkesley Sir Knight(이)가 1357년 영국 노르스벌랜드 캡히턴에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 William de Swinburne(은)는 88세였고, 그의 어머니 Philippa de Gernon(은)는 88세였다. 그는 1357년 8월 이전 영국 잉글랜드에서 Agnes de Felton(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 한 명의 아들과 세 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1391년 10월 6일 영국 에세스 리틀 호르케슬리에서 34세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 잉글랜드 에세스 리틀 호르케슬리에 묻혔다.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Lord Robert de Swinburne of Little Horkesley Sir Knight родился в 1357 г. в местности кафитон, нортумбирланд, Англия, его отцу по имени William de Swinburne было 88 лет, а матери, которую звали Philippa de Gernon, 88. Он женился на женщине по имени Agnes de Felton перед августа 1357 г. в местности Англия, Великобритания. У них был по крайней мере 1 сын и 3 дочери. Он умер 6 октября 1391 г. в местности литл хауркесли, эссекс, Англия в возрасте 34 лет и был похоронен в местности литл хауркесли, эссекс, Англия, Великобритания.
